title: What is a Struct? 🧱
description: Group different data types into one object.

slides.title: The Limitations of Arrays 🧱
slides.content:
Arrays are great for storing lists, but they have a fatal flaw: **Every item in an array must be the exact same data type.**

What if you want to store information about a `Player`? You need an `int` for health, a `char array` for their name, and a `float` for their speed. You can't put those into one array.

slides.title: Enter the Struct
slides.content: A **Structure (`struct`)** allows you to invent your own custom data type by bundling different variables together under one group name.

quizData.question: Which operator is used to access the individual variables (members) stored inside a `struct` object?
quizData.explanation: You use a dot `.` followed by the member name (like `p1.health`) to read or write to that specific part of the struct.

slides.title: A Blueprint, Not a House 🏠
slides.content:
Writing `struct Player { ... };` does NOT consume any memory or create a player.

It is purely a **Blueprint** thrown to the compiler. You don't actually consume any memory until you explicitly build a house from that blueprint inside `main()` by writing `struct Player myPlayer;`.

quizData.question: A struct is not a variable. You cannot assign a value like `health = 100;` directly inside the blueprint definition.
quizData.explanation: You can't give a blueprint a health score. You have to build the player `p1` first, and then give `p1` a health score.
